summary: This part of the course was really about learning one universal process for turning any resource-allocation problem into math. The 4-step LP formulation — variables, objective, constraints, non-negativity — is the same structure whether you're scheduling staff, planning production, or allocating a budget.
keyIdeas: The 4-step process: define decision variables → write objective function → write constraints → add non-negativity
Objective functions maximize (profit, return) or minimize (cost, time) — direction matters
Constraint directions encode business meaning: resource limits use ≤, minimum requirements use ≥, exact balances use =
Shadow prices reveal the marginal value of relaxing a binding constraint — the price of flexibility
Sensitivity ranges tell you how much an input coefficient can change before the optimal solution itself changes
practicalIntuition: LP is the foundation of prescriptive analytics in industry — production planning, supply chain optimization, workforce scheduling, and portfolio allocation all use LP daily. Understanding formulation is what separates someone who runs a solver from someone who designs the model.
commonConfusion: Getting constraint inequality directions backwards — resource limits use ≤ (you can't exceed capacity), but minimum requirements use ≥ (you must meet at least this much). Flipping them produces the wrong feasible region.
assignmentAnchor: Assignment 2: RRB Watercraft LP formulation (from exam prep materials)
businessMeaning: Every business faces constrained resource allocation. LP provides a mathematically rigorous way to find the optimal solution — not just a good one.
sourceDeck: MBAN 5540 exam prep materials (provisional reconstruction — no slides extracted)

summary: This part of the course extended LP to real-world decisions that are inherently discrete. Binary variables let you model yes/no choices — open a warehouse or not, fund a project or not — and facility location added linking constraints that enforce logical consistency between what you build and what you use.
keyIdeas: Binary variables (0 or 1) model go/no-go decisions — LP can't handle these, IP can
The knapsack problem: maximize value subject to capacity — foundational structure for project selection and capital budgeting
Assignment problems: match n agents to n tasks one-to-one at minimum cost — solved as IP or as transportation networks
Facility location requires linking constraints: x_ij ≤ y_j means you can only assign customer i to facility j if facility j is open (y_j = 1)
IP is NP-hard — as problem size grows, solver time can become unpredictable. Real-world IP requires knowing when to approximate
practicalIntuition: Most real strategic decisions are binary: open or close a location, fund or pass on a project, hire or don't. LP can't handle these. IP is what makes prescriptive analytics applicable to actual business decisions rather than just toy allocation problems.
commonConfusion: Forgetting linking constraints in facility location models. Without x_ij ≤ y_j, the solver will happily assign customers to facilities that aren't open — the solution looks optimal but is operationally nonsense.
assignmentAnchor: Assignment 3: Multiple Knapsack, Assignment Problem, Transshipment network
businessMeaning: Project portfolio selection, warehouse network design, capital budgeting, and route optimization all require integer programming. This is where LP models become enterprise decision tools.
sourceDeck: MBAN 5540 exam prep materials (provisional reconstruction — no slides extracted)

summary: This part of the course was about making structured decisions when you don't know what will happen — using expected monetary value, decision trees, and Bayesian updating to turn uncertainty into calculable risk. The exam-prep Nuclear War Manual made these techniques concrete through worked problems.
keyIdeas: EMV (Expected Monetary Value) = Σ(probability × payoff for each outcome) — choose the alternative with the highest EMV
Decision trees fold back right-to-left: chance nodes take expected values, decision nodes take the max (or min for costs)
EVPI = EVwPI − EMV* — this is the maximum you should ever pay for perfect information. It's a ceiling, not a target
Bayesian updating: P(state | signal) = P(signal | state) × P(state) / P(signal) — posteriors must sum to 1.0
EVSI (Expected Value of Sample Information) measures the value of imperfect information like a market research study
practicalIntuition: Every major business decision under uncertainty — product launches, investment decisions, R&D commitments — can be framed as a decision tree. The framework forces you to be explicit about probabilities and payoffs instead of relying on gut feel.
commonConfusion: Treating EVPI as a target rather than a ceiling. EVPI tells you the theoretical maximum benefit of perfect information. No real information is perfect, so your actual willingness-to-pay should always be less than EVPI.
assignmentAnchor: Assignment 5: Decision analysis with Bayesian updating (Nuclear War Manual Q1-Q5)
businessMeaning: Investment committees, product teams, and strategic planners all benefit from formal decision analysis. Quantifying uncertainty forces clearer thinking and more defensible recommendations.
sourceDeck: MBAN 5540 Nuclear War Manual (exam prep materials, provisional reconstruction)

summary: The final analytical layer in 5540: dynamic programming for multi-stage optimization (work backwards from the end state) and Markov chains for systems that evolve randomly over time. Both appeared in the Nuclear War Manual exam prep.
keyIdeas: Dynamic programming solves multi-stage problems by working backwards: start at the last stage, compute the optimal decision, propagate backward to stage 1
The Bellman equation: f*(s) = max/min { immediate reward at s + f*(next state) } — this recursive structure is the entire DP algorithm
DTMC (Discrete-Time Markov Chain): transition matrix P where rows sum to 1. Steady-state: solve πP = π with Σπᵢ = 1
CTMC (Continuous-Time Markov Chain): uses generator matrix Q where rows sum to 0 instead of 1 — different matrix, different solution method
Steady-state probabilities describe long-run system behavior — essential for capacity planning, queuing, and reliability analysis
practicalIntuition: Markov chains model systems where the future depends only on the present state, not the full history — queuing systems, customer lifecycle stages, equipment reliability, and inventory levels all have this property. Knowing steady-state behavior lets you size systems correctly.
commonConfusion: Confusing DTMC and CTMC at the matrix level: DTMC transition matrix P has rows summing to 1 (probability distributions). CTMC generator matrix Q has rows summing to 0 (rates, not probabilities). Different matrix types require different solution approaches.
assignmentAnchor: Exam preparation: Nuclear War Manual Q6-Q8 (DP and Markov chain problems)
businessMeaning: Queue management, inventory replenishment, customer lifecycle modeling, and infrastructure reliability engineering all use Markov chain steady-state analysis. DP underpins sequential decision problems in supply chain, pricing, and scheduling.
sourceDeck: MBAN 5540 Nuclear War Manual (exam prep materials, provisional reconstruction)
